query: Which character comes from Bangor, Maine?

retrieved_chunks.chunk:
Transcribed from the 1887 Macmillan and Co. edition by David Price, email
ccx074@coventry.ac.uk.  Proofing by Andy McLauchan and David Stapleton.
A BUNDLE OF LETTERS
by Henry James
CHAPTER I
FROM MISS MIRANDA MOPE, IN PARIS, TO MRS. ABRAHAM C. MOPE, AT BANGOR,
MAINE.
September 5th, 1879.
My dear mother--I have kept you posted as far as Tuesday week last, and,
although my letter will not have reached you yet, I will begin another
before my news accumulates too much.  I am glad you show my letters round
in the family, for I like them all to know what I am doing, and I can't
write to every one, though I try to answer all reasonable expectations.
But there are a great many unreasonable ones, as I suppose you know--not
yours, dear mother, for I am bound to say that you never required of me
more than was natural.  You see you are reaping your reward: I write to
you before I write to any one else.
There is one thing, I hope--that you don't show any of my letters to
William Platt.  If he wants to see any of my letters, he knows the right
way to go to work.  I wouldn't have him see one of these letters, written
for circulation in the family, for anything in the world.  If he wants
one for himself, he has got to write to me first.  Let him write to me
first, and then I will see about answering him.  You can show him this if
you like; but if you show him anything more, I will never write to you
again.
I told you in my last about my farewell to England, my crossing the
Channel, and my first impressions of Paris.  I have thought a great deal
about that lovely England since I left it, and all the famous historic
scenes I visited; but I have come to the conclusion that it is not a
country in which I should care to reside.  The position of woman does not
seem to me at all satisfactory, and that is a point, you know, on which I
feel very strongly.  It seems to me that in England they play a very
faded-out part, and those with whom I conversed had a kind of depressed
and humiliated tone; a little dull, tame look, as if they were used to
being snubbed and bullied, which made me want to give them a good
shaking.  There are a great many people--and a great many things,
too--over here that I should like to perform that operation upon.  I
should like to shake the starch out of some of them, and the dust out of
the others.  I know fifty girls in Bangor that come much more up to my
notion of the stand a truly noble woman should take, than those young
ladies in England.  But they had a most lovely way of speaking (in
England), and the men are _remarkably handsome_.  (You can show this to
William Platt, if you like.)

I have received a great deal of politeness--some of it really most
pressing, and I have experienced no drawbacks whatever.  I have made a
great many pleasant acquaintances in travelling round (both ladies and
gentlemen), and had a great many most interesting talks.  I have
collected a great deal of information, for which I refer you to my
journal.  I assure you my journal is going to be a splendid thing.  I do
just exactly as I do in Bangor, and I find I do perfectly right; and at
any rate, I don't care if I don't.  I didn't come to Europe to lead a
merely conventional life; I could do that at Bangor.  You know I never
_would_ do it at Bangor, so it isn't likely I am going to make myself
miserable over here.  So long as I accomplish what I desire, and make my
money hold out, I shall regard the thing as a success.  Sometimes I feel
rather lonely, especially in the evening; but I generally manage to
interest myself in something or in some one.  In the evening I usually
read up about the objects of interest I have visited during the day, or I
post up my journal.  Sometimes I go to the theatre; or else I play the
piano in the public parlour.  The public parlour at the hotel isn't much;
but the piano is better than that fearful old thing at the Sebago House.
Sometimes I go downstairs and talk to the lady who keeps the books--a
French lady, who is remarkably polite.  She is very pretty, and always
wears a black dress, with the most beautiful fit; she speaks a little
English; she tells me she had to learn it in order to converse with the
Americans who come in such numbers to this hotel.  She has given me a
great deal of information about the position of woman in France, and much
of it is very encouraging.  But she has told me at the same time some
things that I should not like to write to you (I am hesitating even about
putting them into my journal), especially if my letters are to be handed
round in the family.  I assure you they appear to talk about things here
that we never think of mentioning at Bangor, or even of thinking about.
She seems to think she can tell me everything, because I told her I was
travelling for general culture.  Well, I _do_ want to know so much that
it seems sometimes as if I wanted to know everything; and yet there are
some things that I think I don't want to know.  But, as a general thing,
everything is intensely interesting; I don't mean only everything that
this French lady tells me, but everything I see and hear for myself.  I
feel really as if I should gain all I desire.

read_agent_gists:
Transcribed by David Price. Proofed by Andy McLauchan and David Stapleton. From "A BUNDLE OF LETTERS" by Henry James.

From Miss Miranda Mope, Paris, to Mrs. Abraham C. Mope, Bangor, Maine, September 5th, 1879.

My dear mother, I am glad you share my letters with the family. I write to you first. Do not show any of my letters to William Platt unless he writes to me first. I miss England but find the position of women there unsatisfactory. They seem depressed and humiliated. I prefer the spirit of the girls in Bangor. The men in England are remarkably handsome.

I shared my first impressions of Paris, which met my expectations. The city is full of interesting sights and has a sunny climate. Women's status is higher but not as high as in America. The people's manners are unique, confirming I'm in foreign parts. Paris is elegant, surpassing New York, and I've spent much time visiting monuments and palaces. I won't detail all my travels since I'm keeping an exhaustive journal for you to read upon my return. I'm doing remarkably well, often surprised by my good fortune. I've found no objections to solo female travel in Europe that were mentioned before I left.

Received much politeness, made pleasant acquaintances, and had interesting talks. Collected information for my journal, which I believe will be splendid. I do as I please, not caring for a conventional life. I feel lonely sometimes but manage to keep myself occupied, reading, journaling, or playing piano. The hotel's piano is better than the one at Sebago House. I talk to the French lady who keeps the books, learning about French culture and women's position. She shares information I hesitate to write about. Overall, everything is intensely interesting, and I feel I will gain all I desire.

Americans are generally less polite to me than Europeans, who are more attentive, especially the gentlemen. I'm unsure if Americans are more sincere. They sometimes express surprise at my solo travel, but I have a standard response: "For general culture, language acquisition, and personal exploration," which usually satisfies them. My funds are holding up well, and it's very interesting.

Since my last letter, I moved to a French family's home that combines a boarding-house and a language school. There are a few others here learning French through conversation. I was glad to come here as I wasn't progressing much with the language. In my previous hotel, I had fewer opportunities to practice French than I did hearing it from French Canadians in Bangor. The hotel owner preferred to speak English, and the staff were mostly not French speakers. Shopping wasn't helping either since I avoid buying things to focus on cultural experiences.

I've been considering getting a teacher but am already familiar with grammar, and teachers focus too much on verbs. I wanted to grasp French conversation before leaving. The theatre offers insight, and I often go alone, always treated politely. I see many other ladies enjoying themselves there too. However, the speed and vulgarity of the conversations are challenging. After visiting the Palais Royal, a renowned theatre, I couldn't understand a word due to the fast pace and unique expressions. Feeling disappointed, I overheard two Americans discussing their goals: one was after French, the other after Art, but both were pursuing French more intensely.

Then, dear mother, I am sorry to say the second one swore a little. He said, "Oh, damn French!" "No, I won't damn French," said his friend. "I'll acquire it--that's what I'll do. I'll go right into a French family. That's the only way to do it. If you want to learn French, you need to immerse yourself. There are families here that take you in and teach you. My cousin got in with one and learned in three months. They just talk to you, and you have to understand them. I mean to find another family, even if it takes a lot of trouble!"

I listened with interest as he spoke about his cousin, but then mentioned they had moved away. The other gentleman was unaffected and discussed the future demand for art in the US, which didn't interest me. I wanted to learn French for its own sake. The next day, I asked the hotel bookkeeper about a family that could help me with conversation. She recommended a family who often hosted Americans learning French and offered to introduce me, but I went alone. They were delighted to receive me, and I was pleased with them. I've seen a lot of them in the past three days. The price is high, but conversation is included. My room is pretty, with seven mirrors, two clocks, and five curtains. I'm disappointed to find several other Americans here for the same reason. I try to talk with Madame de Maisonrouge as much as I can; she and her daughters are elegant and interesting.

September 21st
From Miss Violet Ray, Paris, to Miss Agnes Rich, New York.

Father got a telegram to return to New York for business, upsetting mother and me. Despite our protests, he insisted we leave Paris, but we refused. Reluctantly, he decided to go alone, leaving us for three months. However, he made us move into a family home instead of staying at the hotel.

Families pretend to teach American and English visitors French but have hidden motives. Mother and I were horrified by this plan, but Father, through persistent nagging, convinced us to try it. He claimed it would be safer, more respectable, and economical, arguing I'd improve my French and Mother would learn French household management. Despite his five hundred reasons, it's all a facade, especially since American business has recovered and fortunes are being made. We've been economizing for five years and hoped to enjoy our benefits abroad.

My French is quite perfect, though I need more practice in genders and idioms. Father, as usual, won't back down; mother deserted me, and after three days, I gave in. He lost three steamers arguing with me. They visited seventeen families and left me in a boarding house. Father insisted on my perfect pronunciation, which I find ironic since that's my strongest area. He'll be gone for three months, and mother and I will breathe easier. Surprisingly, the boarding house is better than expected, though living with strangers is unpleasant.

Our rooms are nicely arranged, and the food is excellent. Mamma finds everything amusing, but I prefer solitude. The place is well-managed, allowing me to do as I please. Madame de Maisonrouge is tactful but I suspect she was once a shop attendant and now hates us, planning to poison us. She has two daughters who mimic her. The "family" includes English and American guests; an affected Englishman, a pretty sister, a pleasant Frenchman, a German doctor, and two Americans, one of whom is a young woman from Vermont or Minnesota, displaying extreme Yankeeism.

CHAPTER IV
September 25th.
My dear Harvard, I've carried out my plan hinted at last and regret not doing it sooner. Human nature is the most interesting thing, revealing itself to earnest seekers. My life of hotels and trains lacked earnestness. I wanted to explore more, but opportunities were always missing. I'm always watching for experiences and adventures.

To live fully, feeling and embracing all possibilities, not just passing through life mechanically, is incredibly attractive. There are moments when I feel capable of both great excesses and heroism. I want experiences that leave a lasting impact, not just second-hand sensations.

If you repeat my remarks to the West Cedar Street circle, tone them down. I have always wanted to experience real French life. I sympathize with the French and their artistic temperament, which Boston lacks. Boston is too focused on right and wrong, making it hard to live aesthetically. I admire the French for their sensuous and aesthetic nature. I regret Theophile Gautier's passing; I would have liked to meet him. When I was here before, I traveled with the non-aesthetic Johnsons, who made me feel ashamed of my artistic side. I prefer to be free and frank, living openly and naively.

With the Johnsons, everything was superficial and focused on right and wrong, making life seem didactic and gray. Now, I've decided to embrace life fully, living in a French family in Paris to experience European life without their prejudices. I have the courage to live as I believe is right.

I have always been fascinated by Balzac's vivid portrayals of Parisian life. Sadly, my French family lives in the less picturesque Boulevard Haussmann, not the historic city center. Despite this, they embody a Balzac-like tone. Madame de Maisonrouge, from an old French family, now runs a boarding house for travelers seeking local color. Unlike the sordid Pension Vauquer, her establishment is elegant and aristocratic, with high windows and refined decor. She reminds me of Madame Hulot, charming yet hinting at hidden complexities.

I am disappointed in the lack of local character in the society here; it is cosmopolitan, featuring French, English, American, German, Russian, and Hungarian individuals. Studying national types and their perspectives is fascinating.

The American types here are uninteresting, except for myself. We are thin, pale, and sharp, lacking richness and temperament. The American temperament is represented by two young girls and one with her mother. These girls are graceful but disappointing, cold, and sexless. One is elegant and expensive from New York, the other plain and pure from New England. They are practical, positive, and subtle, yet I enjoy talking and studying them.

The New Yorker finds Boston amusing and often mocks its intellectual reputation. She travels alone in Europe, unaffected by its complexities. In contrast, an English girl with a sweet demeanor and stylish, intricate attire represents a revival of beauty in England. She is deeply picturesque and more traditionally feminine than the New Yorker. Her brother, a fair-haired Englishman, complements her grace.

September 26th. Don't worry if you don't hear from me often; I'm doing well and very busy. It's a blessing to be here, where I can do significant work despite challenges. With only a year in Europe, I value every hour.

I face challenges learning French despite being in a French family, with English spoken all around. Undeterred, I practice French daily, including lessons with Miss Maisonrouge and evening conversations with Madame and her friends. I also have extra private lessons and walks with Mr. Verdier, a handsome and polite French gentleman. We plan to visit galleries and attend the opera together. His compliments, though possibly insincere, are numerous.

The conversation in the parlour is often brilliant, and I wish you could hear it. At Bangor, people seem to express less, giving up before trying. Here, I've learned to express everything, which might make them think I'm not sincere when I return. I've become friends with everyone except an American girl from New York who is remarkably pretty but refuses to associate with me. I'm curious about her reason for this behavior and determined to find out before I leave.

A gentleman from Boston, Mr. Louis Leverett, is highly refined despite looking sick from a liver condition. His conversations are interesting and intellectual, often including French expressions. Unlike Mr. Verdier, Mr. Leverett is less complimentary but more thoughtful. He loves pictures and has expanded the narrator's understanding of them, believing they are underappreciated, especially in Bangor.

If I had money, I'd buy pictures to hang up. Mr. Leverett thinks it would benefit Bangor folks and praises the French, saying we don't appreciate them enough. I told him the French make enough of themselves. He talks about the French enthusiastically, which is interesting and beneficial for me. I talk to him about Boston but feel guilty for not focusing solely on European culture. I can get Boston culture later if I move there, but I can't help peeking ahead with Mr. Leverett. He's full of culture, and meeting others like him will ensure I won't miss out when I achieve my dream.

There are two English people, very cultivated, whose way of speaking I admire. I love how they pronounce words and the sweetness of their voices. They seem to try a lot to talk like that, but here, they don't seem to try at all. They are a young lady and her brother from a noble family. I've had a lot of interaction with them because I feel more free to talk to them than to Americans, almost learning a new language in the process.

I never supposed, when I left Bangor, that I was coming to Europe to learn English! If I do, they won't understand me back home. Bangor is the most critical place I know. Tell them they are too fastidious. I wish I could show you the English young lady and her brother. She's lovely but dresses in a way that attracts great attention, which she doesn't seem to notice. Mr. Leverett thinks it's the "costume of the future," but I call it the "costume of the past." Madame do Maisonrouge laughed when I mentioned it.

I tried to understand women's position in England and asked Miss Vane for information. She said a lady's status depends on her father, eldest brother, or husband's rank. Her own status is good because her father is related to a lord. She values this highly, which suggests the position of women isn't satisfactory since it relies on family status. I find it frustrating to hear her talk about lords as if I should know about them.

I feel compelled to ask her if she considers everyone equal, but she insists she isn't equal to Lady Something-or-other, a relation of her father. Despite my efforts to persuade her, she remains unconvinced and defends Lady So-and-so, calling her "extremely nice." I find this attitude troubling and consider it vulgar. Miss Vane is also very afraid of her brother, which influences her behavior and thoughts, showing that timidity can't be reasoned away.

Mr. Vane, despite being told his sister is his equal, laughs off such claims. A German professor, seated next to the narrator, shows genuine interest and often discusses these topics post-dinner. The professor, described as highly educated and observant, leaves the narrator feeling mentally refreshed. The narrator ponders staying longer but feels the weather is dull and damp, sometimes wishing for colder conditions.
From Miss Evelyn Vane, Paris, to Lady Augusta Fleming, Brighton. September 30th.

Dear Lady Augusta, I can't visit on January 7th as planned in Homburg. Mamma and the children are coming to Hyeres for Georgina's health, despite her reluctance and poor behavior. Hyeres is economical but may not suit us all due to the intense heat. I hope it helps Georgina, though she's been wilful and uncooperative.

Mamma is taking Mary, Gus, Fred, and Adelaide abroad; the others will stay at Kingscote until February, then go to Eastbourne with the new governess, Miss Turnover, for a month. Miss Travers, who's only qualified for younger children, is going to Hyeres. Mamma considered asking Miss Travers to change her name but papa thought she might object. Lady Battledown makes all her governesses use the name Johnson for 5 pounds more a year. I stayed in Paris for a month with Harold to "finish" my French before joining them.

Harold came six weeks ago to prepare for French exams, living with a French family recommended by Mamma. She asked me to join him. The Desmonds arranged it; Harold was not pleased but has been kind. He's excelling in French despite the place not meeting Papa's expectations. I learn less but don't have exams like Harold does. Mamma might test me, but I hope she's too busy with Georgina to do so. If she does, I'll be very nervous.

This place isn't as nice for a girl as for a young man. The Desmonds found it odd that my mother wanted me to come here, considering her unconventional nature. Paris is amusing, but I'll wait for the caravan. The establishment's owner is odd and foreign but civil. The servants are unlike English ones, often late but sometimes bursting in unexpectedly. It's uncomfortable, and I expect it will be worse at Hyeres. There are odd Americans here, including a madman who talks about the sky and turf, and two girls, one of whom is nice but has a vulgar mother. The other girl is a schoolmistress with radical views. There's also a nasty Frenchman and a boring German professor. I'm sorry about canceling my visit; I hope you'll still ask me again.

CHAPTER VII

From Leon Verdier, Paris, to Prosper Gobain, Lille.
Sept 28th.

My Dear Prosper,
It's been a while since I last shared my news. I'm happy now, and I think of you often. In the good old days, I always looked forward to sharing my adventures with you. Today, I am blissful, surrounded by three lovely girls. I hope you can imagine my joy.

Best,
Leon

You expected me to talk about my fortune or Uncle Blondeau's legacy, but women play a significant role in both happiness and misery. I'll now tell you about _ces demoiselles_. My cousin de Maisonrouge, a grande belle femme, after her second marriage, found herself in financial trouble with two daughters to raise. She opened a pension for foreign students, which became successful. Recently, she asked me to stay with her for free to help with the students' grammar. I'm the pension's new addition, living off her hospitality and enjoying the company of pretty English students.

Just now, I converse daily with three pairs. One owner pays extra for private lessons. My cousin doesn't pay me, but I'm compensated. I'm well with the other two owners. One is a twenty-year-old Anglaise, the most adorable miss I've seen, with soft gray eyes and a delicate blush.

She pretends to be prim in public but is more relaxed in private. I've offered her support, and while she appreciates it, progress is slow due to her reserved nature. Meanwhile, the Americans are more eager, moving quickly into intimate encounters. One American, particularly bold, initiated a rendezvous within twelve minutes. Another, more reserved, prefers private meetings in the afternoon. Overall, the private lessons with the tall American are the most engaging, despite her plain appearance. She's curious about the world and unafraid to show it.

CHAPTER VIII
FROM DR. RUDOLF STAUB, IN PARIS, TO DR. JULIUS HIRSCH, AT GOTTINGEN.
My dear brother in Science--I continue my notes from weeks ago. I left my hotel, feeling it wasn't sufficiently local, and decided to immerse myself more in Parisian life to better understand the factors contributing to its intellectual activity.

I rented a room from a French lady who supplements her income by hosting distinguished guests. She didn't accept my offer to dine elsewhere, but I've used the opportunity to study my companions' table manners and the French nature during meals, adjusting my observations keenly.

Unfortunately, I find the individual native in the minority. There are four French persons in the house: three women and one man. This preponderance of women is characteristic, given their significant role in French history. The man seems less human than simian, and his speech sounds like a hand-organ with a hairy _homunculus_ accompanying it.

My expectation of rough treatment due to my German nationality proved unfounded. No one seems to know or care about my nationality, and I am treated with civility. This has surprised me, as I had assumed my presence would irritate the French, prompting a clear expression of their character. Instead, I am treated no differently from others, which contradicts my initial hypothesis. I believe the French are so self-focused that they have forgotten the concept of Germans, considering them irrelevant. Thus, the idea of revenge is too complex for the French to seriously consider.

I have examined English-speaking specimens, focusing on American varieties, finding several unique examples. Notably, a young man embodies national decline, reminiscent of a small, Hellenized Roman from the third century. This reflects a cultural period where appreciation surpasses production, leading to mental sterility. He proudly informed me that Boston is largely composed of similar individuals, unaware of the negative impression this created.

The passage describes an unprecedented phenomenon where American society skips a developmental stage, moving directly from crudity to decay without a period of maturity. This is illustrated through encounters with young Americans who exhibit signs of moral decay and internal strife, suggesting a premature decline of the American population. The author predicts this could lead to the decline of English-speaking societies, potentially benefiting others like the Germans.

CHAPTER IX  
MIRANDA TO HER MOTHER  
October 22d  
Dear Mother, I'm leaving soon for a new country, undecided yet. I've learned French well and enjoyed my stay with Madame de Maisonrouge and Mr. Verdier, who I'll correspond with. The German gentleman is fascinating. I upset the New Yorker by admiring the Louvre. William Platt's letter arrived. My money is fine, and it's very interesting!
system_prompt: You are a careful research assistant. Answer the question concisely and based only on the provided context. When the answer appears explicitly in the context, copy the exact answer text rather than paraphrasing. If the answer is short, reply with the answer only instead of a full sentence.

by Henry James

MAINE.
September 5th, 1879.
My dear mother--I have kept you posted as far as Tuesday week last, and,
although my letter will not have reached you yet, I will begin another
before my news accumulates too much.  I am glad you show my letters round
in the family, for I like them all to know what I am doing, and I can't
write to every one, though I try to answer all reasonable expectations.

The theatre offers insight, and I often go alone, always treated politely. I see many other ladies enjoying themselves there too. However, the speed and vulgarity of the conversations are challenging. After visiting the Palais Royal, a renowned theatre, I couldn't understand a word due to the fast pace and unique expressions. Feeling disappointed, I overheard two Americans discussing their goals: one was after French, the other after Art, but both were pursuing French more intensely.
Then, dear mother, I am sorry to say the second one swore a little. He said, "Oh, damn French!" "No, I won't damn French," said his friend. "I'll acquire it--that's what I'll do. I'll go right into a French family. That's the only way to do it. If you want to learn French, you need to immerse yourself. There are families here that take you in and teach you. My cousin got in with one and learned in three months. They just talk to you, and you have to understand them. I mean to find another family, even if it takes a lot of trouble!"
I listened with interest as he spoke about his cousin, but then mentioned they had moved away. The other gentleman was unaffected and discussed the future demand for art in the US, which didn't interest me. I wanted to learn French for its own sake. The next day, I asked the hotel bookkeeper about a family that could help me with conversation. She recommended a family who often hosted Americans learning French and offered to introduce me, but I went alone. They were delighted to receive me, and I was pleased with them. I've seen a lot of them in the past three days. The price is high, but conversation is included. My room is pretty, with seven mirrors, two clocks, and five curtains. I'm disappointed to find several other Americans here for the same reason. I try to talk with Madame de Maisonrouge as much as I can; she and her daughters are elegant and interesting.
September 21st
From Miss Violet Ray, Paris, to Miss Agnes Rich, New York.
Father got a telegram to return to New York for business, upsetting mother and me. Despite our protests, he insisted we leave Paris, but we refused. Reluctantly, he decided to go alone, leaving us for three months. However, he made us move into a family home instead of staying at the hotel.
Families pretend to teach American and English visitors French but have hidden motives. Mother and I were horrified by this plan, but Father, through persistent nagging, convinced us to try it. He claimed it would be safer, more respectable, and economical, arguing I'd improve my French and Mother would learn French household management. Despite his five hundred reasons, it's all a facade, especially since American business has recovered and fortunes are being made. We've been economizing for five years and hoped to enjoy our benefits abroad.
My French is quite perfect, though I need more practice in genders and idioms. Father, as usual, won't back down; mother deserted me, and after three days, I gave in. He lost three steamers arguing with me. They visited seventeen families and left me in a boarding house. Father insisted on my perfect pronunciation, which I find ironic since that's my strongest area. He'll be gone for three months, and mother and I will breathe easier. Surprisingly, the boarding house is better than expected, though living with strangers is unpleasant.
Our rooms are nicely arranged, and the food is excellent. Mamma finds everything amusing, but I prefer solitude. The place is well-managed, allowing me to do as I please. Madame de Maisonrouge is tactful but I suspect she was once a shop attendant and now hates us, planning to poison us. She has two daughters who mimic her. The "family" includes English and American guests; an affected Englishman, a pretty sister, a pleasant Frenchman, a German doctor, and two Americans, one of whom is a young woman from Vermont or Minnesota, displaying extreme Yankeeism.
CHAPTER IV
September 25th.
My dear Harvard, I've carried out my plan hinted at last and regret not doing it sooner. Human nature is the most interesting thing, revealing itself to earnest seekers. My life of hotels and trains lacked earnestness. I wanted to explore more, but opportunities were always missing. I'm always watching for experiences and adventures.
To live fully, feeling and embracing all possibilities, not just passing through life mechanically, is incredibly attractive. There are moments when I feel capable of both great excesses and heroism. I want experiences that leave a lasting impact, not just second-hand sensations.
If you repeat my remarks to the West Cedar Street circle, tone them down. I have always wanted to experience real French life. I sympathize with the French and their artistic temperament, which Boston lacks. Boston is too focused on right and wrong, making it hard to live aesthetically. I admire the French for their sensuous and aesthetic nature. I regret Theophile Gautier's passing; I would have liked to meet him. When I was here before, I traveled with the non-aesthetic Johnsons, who made me feel ashamed of my artistic side. I prefer to be free and frank, living openly and naively.
With the Johnsons, everything was superficial and focused on right and wrong, making life seem didactic and gray. Now, I've decided to embrace life fully, living in a French family in Paris to experience European life without their prejudices. I have the courage to live as I believe is right.
I have always been fascinated by Balzac's vivid portrayals of Parisian life. Sadly, my French family lives in the less picturesque Boulevard Haussmann, not the historic city center. Despite this, they embody a Balzac-like tone. Madame de Maisonrouge, from an old French family, now runs a boarding house for travelers seeking local color. Unlike the sordid Pension Vauquer, her establishment is elegant and aristocratic, with high windows and refined decor. She reminds me of Madame Hulot, charming yet hinting at hidden complexities.
I am disappointed in the lack of local character in the society here; it is cosmopolitan, featuring French, English, American, German, Russian, and Hungarian individuals. Studying national types and their perspectives is fascinating.
The American types here are uninteresting, except for myself. We are thin, pale, and sharp, lacking richness and temperament. The American temperament is represented by two young girls and one with her mother. These girls are graceful but disappointing, cold, and sexless. One is elegant and expensive from New York, the other plain and pure from New England. They are practical, positive, and subtle, yet I enjoy talking and studying them.
The New Yorker finds Boston amusing and often mocks its intellectual reputation. She travels alone in Europe, unaffected by its complexities. In contrast, an English girl with a sweet demeanor and stylish, intricate attire represents a revival of beauty in England. She is deeply picturesque and more traditionally feminine than the New Yorker. Her brother, a fair-haired Englishman, complements her grace.
September 26th. Don't worry if you don't hear from me often; I'm doing well and very busy. It's a blessing to be here, where I can do significant work despite challenges. With only a year in Europe, I value every hour.
I face challenges learning French despite being in a French family, with English spoken all around. Undeterred, I practice French daily, including lessons with Miss Maisonrouge and evening conversations with Madame and her friends. I also have extra private lessons and walks with Mr. Verdier, a handsome and polite French gentleman. We plan to visit galleries and attend the opera together. His compliments, though possibly insincere, are numerous.
The conversation in the parlour is often brilliant, and I wish you could hear it. At Bangor, people seem to express less, giving up before trying. Here, I've learned to express everything, which might make them think I'm not sincere when I return. I've become friends with everyone except an American girl from New York who is remarkably pretty but refuses to associate with me. I'm curious about her reason for this behavior and determined to find out before I leave.
A gentleman from Boston, Mr. Louis Leverett, is highly refined despite looking sick from a liver condition. His conversations are interesting and intellectual, often including French expressions. Unlike Mr. Verdier, Mr. Leverett is less complimentary but more thoughtful. He loves pictures and has expanded the narrator's understanding of them, believing they are underappreciated, especially in Bangor.
If I had money, I'd buy pictures to hang up. Mr. Leverett thinks it would benefit Bangor folks and praises the French, saying we don't appreciate them enough. I told him the French make enough of themselves. He talks about the French enthusiastically, which is interesting and beneficial for me. I talk to him about Boston but feel guilty for not focusing solely on European culture. I can get Boston culture later if I move there, but I can't help peeking ahead with Mr. Leverett. He's full of culture, and meeting others like him will ensure I won't miss out when I achieve my dream.
There are two English people, very cultivated, whose way of speaking I admire. I love how they pronounce words and the sweetness of their voices. They seem to try a lot to talk like that, but here, they don't seem to try at all. They are a young lady and her brother from a noble family. I've had a lot of interaction with them because I feel more free to talk to them than to Americans, almost learning a new language in the process.
I never supposed, when I left Bangor, that I was coming to Europe to learn English! If I do, they won't understand me back home. Bangor is the most critical place I know. Tell them they are too fastidious. I wish I could show you the English young lady and her brother. She's lovely but dresses in a way that attracts great attention, which she doesn't seem to notice. Mr. Leverett thinks it's the "costume of the future," but I call it the "costume of the past." Madame do Maisonrouge laughed when I mentioned it.
I tried to understand women's position in England and asked Miss Vane for information. She said a lady's status depends on her father, eldest brother, or husband's rank. Her own status is good because her father is related to a lord. She values this highly, which suggests the position of women isn't satisfactory since it relies on family status. I find it frustrating to hear her talk about lords as if I should know about them.
I feel compelled to ask her if she considers everyone equal, but she insists she isn't equal to Lady Something-or-other, a relation of her father. Despite my efforts to persuade her, she remains unconvinced and defends Lady So-and-so, calling her "extremely nice." I find this attitude troubling and consider it vulgar. Miss Vane is also very afraid of her brother, which influences her behavior and thoughts, showing that timidity can't be reasoned away.
Mr. Vane, despite being told his sister is his equal, laughs off such claims. A German professor, seated next to the narrator, shows genuine interest and often discusses these topics post-dinner. The professor, described as highly educated and observant, leaves the narrator feeling mentally refreshed. The narrator ponders staying longer but feels the weather is dull and damp, sometimes wishing for colder conditions.
From Miss Evelyn Vane, Paris, to Lady Augusta Fleming, Brighton. September 30th.
Dear Lady Augusta, I can't visit on January 7th as planned in Homburg. Mamma and the children are coming to Hyeres for Georgina's health, despite her reluctance and poor behavior. Hyeres is economical but may not suit us all due to the intense heat. I hope it helps Georgina, though she's been wilful and uncooperative.
Mamma is taking Mary, Gus, Fred, and Adelaide abroad; the others will stay at Kingscote until February, then go to Eastbourne with the new governess, Miss Turnover, for a month. Miss Travers, who's only qualified for younger children, is going to Hyeres. Mamma considered asking Miss Travers to change her name but papa thought she might object. Lady Battledown makes all her governesses use the name Johnson for 5 pounds more a year. I stayed in Paris for a month with Harold to "finish" my French before joining them.
Harold came six weeks ago to prepare for French exams, living with a French family recommended by Mamma. She asked me to join him. The Desmonds arranged it; Harold was not pleased but has been kind. He's excelling in French despite the place not meeting Papa's expectations. I learn less but don't have exams like Harold does. Mamma might test me, but I hope she's too busy with Georgina to do so. If she does, I'll be very nervous.
This place isn't as nice for a girl as for a young man. The Desmonds found it odd that my mother wanted me to come here, considering her unconventional nature. Paris is amusing, but I'll wait for the caravan. The establishment's owner is odd and foreign but civil. The servants are unlike English ones, often late but sometimes bursting in unexpectedly. It's uncomfortable, and I expect it will be worse at Hyeres. There are odd Americans here, including a madman who talks about the sky and turf, and two girls, one of whom is nice but has a vulgar mother. The other girl is a schoolmistress with radical views. There's also a nasty Frenchman and a boring German professor. I'm sorry about canceling my visit; I hope you'll still ask me again.
CHAPTER VII

From Leon Verdier, Paris, to Prosper Gobain, Lille.
Sept 28th.

My Dear Prosper,
It's been a while since I last shared my news. I'm happy now, and I think of you often. In the good old days, I always looked forward to sharing my adventures with you. Today, I am blissful, surrounded by three lovely girls. I hope you can imagine my joy.

Best,
Leon
You expected me to talk about my fortune or Uncle Blondeau's legacy, but women play a significant role in both happiness and misery. I'll now tell you about _ces demoiselles_. My cousin de Maisonrouge, a grande belle femme, after her second marriage, found herself in financial trouble with two daughters to raise. She opened a pension for foreign students, which became successful. Recently, she asked me to stay with her for free to help with the students' grammar. I'm the pension's new addition, living off her hospitality and enjoying the company of pretty English students.
Just now, I converse daily with three pairs. One owner pays extra for private lessons. My cousin doesn't pay me, but I'm compensated. I'm well with the other two owners. One is a twenty-year-old Anglaise, the most adorable miss I've seen, with soft gray eyes and a delicate blush.
She pretends to be prim in public but is more relaxed in private. I've offered her support, and while she appreciates it, progress is slow due to her reserved nature. Meanwhile, the Americans are more eager, moving quickly into intimate encounters. One American, particularly bold, initiated a rendezvous within twelve minutes. Another, more reserved, prefers private meetings in the afternoon. Overall, the private lessons with the tall American are the most engaging, despite her plain appearance. She's curious about the world and unafraid to show it.
CHAPTER VIII
FROM DR. RUDOLF STAUB, IN PARIS, TO DR. JULIUS HIRSCH, AT GOTTINGEN.
My dear brother in Science--I continue my notes from weeks ago. I left my hotel, feeling it wasn't sufficiently local, and decided to immerse myself more in Parisian life to better understand the factors contributing to its intellectual activity.
I rented a room from a French lady who supplements her income by hosting distinguished guests. She didn't accept my offer to dine elsewhere, but I've used the opportunity to study my companions' table manners and the French nature during meals, adjusting my observations keenly.
Unfortunately, I find the individual native in the minority. There are four French persons in the house: three women and one man. This preponderance of women is characteristic, given their significant role in French history. The man seems less human than simian, and his speech sounds like a hand-organ with a hairy _homunculus_ accompanying it.
My expectation of rough treatment due to my German nationality proved unfounded. No one seems to know or care about my nationality, and I am treated with civility. This has surprised me, as I had assumed my presence would irritate the French, prompting a clear expression of their character. Instead, I am treated no differently from others, which contradicts my initial hypothesis. I believe the French are so self-focused that they have forgotten the concept of Germans, considering them irrelevant. Thus, the idea of revenge is too complex for the French to seriously consider.
I have examined English-speaking specimens, focusing on American varieties, finding several unique examples. Notably, a young man embodies national decline, reminiscent of a small, Hellenized Roman from the third century. This reflects a cultural period where appreciation surpasses production, leading to mental sterility. He proudly informed me that Boston is largely composed of similar individuals, unaware of the negative impression this created.
The passage describes an unprecedented phenomenon where American society skips a developmental stage, moving directly from crudity to decay without a period of maturity. This is illustrated through encounters with young Americans who exhibit signs of moral decay and internal strife, suggesting a premature decline of the American population. The author predicts this could lead to the decline of English-speaking societies, potentially benefiting others like the Germans.
CHAPTER IX  
MIRANDA TO HER MOTHER  
October 22d  
Dear Mother, I'm leaving soon for a new country, undecided yet. I've learned French well and enjoyed my stay with Madame de Maisonrouge and Mr. Verdier, who I'll correspond with. The German gentleman is fascinating. I upset the New Yorker by admiring the Louvre. William Platt's letter arrived. My money is fine, and it's very interesting!

Question: Which character comes from Bangor, Maine?

Answer concisely and based only on the provided context. If the answer appears verbatim in the context, copy that exact text. If the answer is short, answer with the answer only, not a full sentence.
